title: Eczema: Causes, Medicinal Herbs and Herbal Preparations
definition: Eczema is a chronic skin condition characterized by dry, itchy, and inflamed patches on the body. It can affect anyone at any age, regardless of their background or lifestyle. For those who suffer from eczema, daily life can become a constant battle to manage its symptoms. Simple tasks like dressing, sleeping, and socializing become challenging due to the relentless itching, stinging, and burning sensations that eczema brings. The condition can also lead to sleep deprivation, as the constant itching makes it difficult to fall or stay asleep. In addition, eczema can cause emotional distress, leading to anxiety, depression, and feelings of self-consciousness about one's appearance. Social events and activities become increasingly difficult due to the need for constant moisturizing and avoiding triggers that can exacerbate symptoms. Even everyday activities like working, studying, or running errands can be affected, as eczema may require frequent breaks to apply creams, take cool baths, or avoid exposure to certain environments. As a result, those with eczema often feel like their condition is taking over their lives, making it difficult to maintain a sense of normalcy and confidence.
definition_desc: Eczema, also known as atopic dermatitis, is a chronic skin condition characterized by dry, itchy, and inflamed skin. It can occur on any part of the body, but is most common on the face, hands, and feet. The symptoms of eczema can be unpredictable and can flare up at any time, causing discomfort, pain, and distress. For those who suffer from eczema, everyday activities can become a challenge. For example, taking a bath can be a nightmare, as the soap and water can further dry out the skin, making the itchiness even worse. Eating favorite foods can be impossible, as certain ingredients can trigger a flare-up, causing swelling, redness, and blistering. Even simple tasks like putting on clothes, tying shoelaces, or even holding a pen can be excruciating due to the constant itching and scratching. Sleeping becomes a luxury, as the itchiness can keep you awake at night, and the constant scratching can leave your skin raw and painful. Social interactions can also be affected, as the constant need to moisturize and manage the condition can make it difficult to participate in social activities, exercise, or even maintain a job. Overall, eczema can significantly impact a person's quality of life, causing stress, anxiety, and emotional distress.
intro_desc: Eczema, also known as atopic dermatitis, is a chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by dry, itchy, and scaly patches on the skin. It's often caused by a combination of genetic and environmental factors, including allergies, stress, and exposure to harsh chemicals. If left untreated, eczema can lead to skin infections, sleep disturbances, and mental health issues. Fortunately, herbal remedies such as aloe vera, calendula, and chamomile can provide relief from eczema symptoms. To use these herbs safely, it's essential to consult with a healthcare professional and follow proper preparation instructions, as excessive use or allergic reactions can occur.
causes_desc: The main causes of eczema are a complex interplay of genetic, environmental, and immune system factors. It is estimated that 80-90% of individuals with eczema have a family history of the condition, indicating a strong genetic predisposition. Allergies, particularly to food and environmental allergens, can trigger eczema symptoms in susceptible individuals. Additionally, skin barrier dysfunction, which allows moisture and allergens to penetrate the skin, is a common feature in eczema patients. Research has also implicated an imbalance of the skin's natural microbiome, with decreased levels of beneficial bacteria such as Staphylococcus and increased levels of pathogenic bacteria.

causes_list.cause_description: Genetics play a significant role in the development of eczema, with individuals who have a family history of the condition being more likely to experience symptoms due to inherited genetic mutations affecting the skin's barrier function and immune response.
Allergies, particularly to food, dust mites, and environmental allergens, can trigger eczema symptoms by causing the immune system to react and leading to inflammation and skin barrier disruption.

Dry skin, often caused by environmental factors such as cold weather, low humidity, and harsh soaps, can lead to eczema symptoms by disrupting the skin's natural barrier function and allowing irritants to penetrate the skin.
Exposure to harsh soaps, detergents, and other cleansers can strip the skin of its natural oils, leading to dryness, irritation, and eczema symptoms.
Stress can trigger eczema symptoms by releasing stress hormones that increase inflammation and disrupt the skin's barrier function, making it more susceptible to irritation and dryness.
Food sensitivities, particularly to dairy, gluten, and soy, can trigger eczema symptoms by causing an immune reaction and leading to inflammation and skin barrier disruption.
Exposure to environmental toxins, such as pesticides, heavy metals, and volatile organic compounds, can trigger eczema symptoms by disrupting the skin's barrier function and causing inflammation.
Hormonal changes during pregnancy, menstruation, and menopause can lead to eczema symptoms by affecting the skin's barrier function and increasing inflammation.
Vitamin deficiencies, particularly in vitamin D and omega-3 fatty acids, can contribute to eczema symptoms by affecting the skin's barrier function and immune response.
Infections, such as bacterial, viral, and fungal infections, can trigger eczema symptoms by causing inflammation and disrupting the skin's barrier function.

herbs_desc: The main medicinal herbs used for eczema are Calendula officinalis, Urtica dioica, Aloe barbadensis, Symphytum officinale, Melaleuca alternifolia, Chamomilla recutita, Avena sativa, Hypericum perforatum, Glycyrrhiza glabra, and Plantago lanceolata. Calendula officinalis has anti-inflammatory properties due to its triterpenoids, which reduce inflammation and promote wound healing. Urtica dioica's beta-sitosterol and ursolic acid exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, alleviating eczema symptoms. Aloe barbadensis contains aloin and aloe-emodin, which soothe and moisturize the skin, reducing inflammation and itching associated with eczema. The combination of these herbs' anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties provides relief from eczema symptoms by reducing inflammation and promoting skin health, making them valuable natural remedies for the condition.

herbs_list.description: Calendula officinalis helps with eczema due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which reduce redness and swelling, and prevent infections in skin lesions.
Urtica dioica helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and oxidative stress through the presence of antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds such as ursolic acid.
Aloe barbadensis helps with eczema by providing soothing and moisturizing properties to the skin, which reduce inflammation, itching, and dryness associated with eczema.
Symphytum officinale helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and promoting skin regeneration through the presence of allantoin, a compound that accelerates wound healing and tissue repair.
Melaleuca alternifolia helps with eczema due to its antimicrobial properties, which prevent infections in skin lesions and reduce inflammation through the presence of compounds such as cineole.
Chamomilla recutita helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and itching through the presence of apigenin and luteolin, flavonoids with anti-inflammatory and antihistamine properties.
Avena sativa helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and itching through the presence of avenanthramides, compounds with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties.
Hypericum perforatum helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and oxidative stress through the presence of hyperforin and hypericin, compounds with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties.
Glycyrrhiza glabra helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and itching through the presence of glycyrrhizin, a compound with anti-inflammatory and antihistamine properties.
Plantago lanceolata helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and oxidative stress through the presence of flavonoids and phenolic acids with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties.

preparations_desc: The main herbal preparations used for eczema are Decoction Cream, Salve, Tincture, Ointment, Infusion, Capsule, Tea, Poultice, Gel, Suppository. Decoction cream is made from boiled herbs and is often used to treat eczema due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties found in plants such as chamomile and calendula. Salve and ointment are typically made from infused oils and herbs like aloe vera, which soothe and moisturize dry skin. Tinctures, made from concentrated plant extracts, are often used topically to treat eczema due to their antihistamine and anti-inflammatory effects found in herbs like St. John's Wort. Infusions, capsules, tea, poultice, gel, and suppository preparations also exist but are less commonly used for eczema treatment due to varying levels of efficacy and potential side effects.
precautions_desc: The main precautions to take before using herbal remedies for eczema relief are to consult with a healthcare professional, especially if you're pregnant, breastfeeding, or taking medications, as some herbal remedies can interact with medications or worsen conditions. Additionally, choose high-quality herbal supplements from reputable manufacturers, as low-quality products can contain contaminants or heavy metals. It's also essential to research and understand the potential allergenic properties of the herbal remedy, as some individuals may experience allergic reactions. Some herbal remedies may also exacerbate eczema symptoms, such as increased inflammation or skin irritation, so monitor your skin closely and discontinue use if adverse effects occur. Furthermore, be aware of potential interactions with other health conditions, such as diabetes or liver disease, and take necessary precautions to avoid adverse effects.

preparations_list.preparation_description: Teas are used to relieve eczema by providing anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce redness and itching, while also promoting relaxation and stress reduction.
Ointments are used to protect and moisturize the skin, creating a barrier against irritants and helping to repair and soothe dry, itchy skin.
Salves are used to lock in moisture and protect the skin from further irritation, reducing the risk of infection and promoting the healing of eczema symptoms.
Creams are used to provide intense moisturization and hydration to the skin, helping to repair and soothe dry, itchy skin and reduce inflammation.
Balms are used to provide long-lasting moisture and protection to the skin, helping to reduce itching and inflammation and promote the healing of eczema symptoms.
Tinctures are used to provide concentrated anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties that help reduce redness, itching, and infection associated with eczema.
Decoctions are used to provide a rich, concentrated liquid that can be applied topically to the skin, helping to reduce inflammation and promote the healing of eczema symptoms.
Poultices are used to apply a thick, paste-like mixture directly to the affected area, providing intense cooling and soothing relief from eczema symptoms.
Compresses are used to apply a cooling, soothing liquid or gel directly to the affected area, helping to reduce itching, inflammation, and pain associated with eczema.
Capsules are used to provide oral supplements that help to reduce inflammation, promote healing, and support the immune system in managing eczema symptoms.

precautions_list.precaution_description: Conduct a patch test on a small area of skin before using any new herbal remedy to check for any adverse reactions or allergic responses, which can cause irritation, inflammation, or even anaphylaxis.
Start with a low dose and gradually increase as needed and under medical supervision to avoid toxicity, interactions with other medications, or over-suppression of the immune system.

Choose reputable and trustworthy sources for herbal remedies to ensure they are free from contaminants, pesticides, heavy metals, or other adulterants that can exacerbate eczema symptoms or cause harm.

Be aware of potential interactions between herbal remedies and prescription medications, including immunosuppressants, corticosteroids, and blood thinners, which can increase the risk of side effects or complications.

Consult a healthcare professional before using herbal remedies during pregnancy or breastfeeding to ensure they are safe for both mother and fetus or baby, as some herbs can stimulate uterine contractions or pass through breast milk.

Be aware of the risk of allergic contact dermatitis, a type of skin reaction that can cause redness, itching, and blistering, particularly when using topical herbal remedies that contain common allergens such as chamomile or calendula.
Avoid using herbal remedies that can increase bleeding risk, such as ginkgo biloba or garlic, before surgery to minimize the risk of complications or excessive bleeding during or after the procedure.
Regularly monitor skin symptoms and adjust herbal remedy use or dosage as needed to prevent over-suppression of the immune system or other adverse effects.

Consult a healthcare professional, particularly a dermatologist or herbalist, before using herbal remedies for eczema to ensure they are safe and effective for individual needs and to obtain personalized guidance and support.

related_ailments_desc: If you have eczema, there's a high chance you also experience dry skin, itchiness, redness, and swelling on the affected areas which can be quite uncomfortable and may disrupt daily activities. Many people with eczema also have asthma, hay fever, or other allergies due to the body's overactive immune response which can trigger inflammation. Some individuals may experience skin infections such as impetigo or cellulitis if bacteria enter the broken skin, leading to pain, swelling, and pus. Additionally, eczema can be associated with other conditions like atopic dermatitis, food allergies, and even mental health issues like anxiety or depression due to the emotional impact of living with a chronic skin condition. Furthermore, people with eczema may also experience sleep disturbances, fatigue, and decreased quality of life due to the persistent itchiness and discomfort.

related_ailments_list.ailment_description: People with eczema often experience asthma due to the underlying inflammation and immune system dysfunction that can contribute to the development of both conditions.
Eczema sufferers often have allergies due to their overactive immune response, which can trigger allergic reactions and exacerbate eczema symptoms.

Psoriasis and eczema can co-occur due to the shared underlying mechanisms, including inflammation and an overactive immune response, which can cause skin cell proliferation and skin thickening in psoriasis.
People with eczema may experience rosacea due to the increased sensitivity of their skin, which can lead to inflammation and redness in response to environmental triggers.

Urticaria, or hives, can occur in people with eczema due to the release of histamine and other chemical mediators from mast cells in response to allergens or irritants, which can cause itchy, raised welts on the skin.
Dermatitis, including contact dermatitis, atopic dermatitis, and seborrheic dermatitis, can co-occur with eczema due to the underlying skin barrier dysfunction and immune system abnormalities.
Atopic anaphylaxis, a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction, can occur in people with eczema due to their overactive immune response and increased sensitivity to allergens.

Pruritus, or intense itching, is a common symptom of eczema, and it can also be a symptom of other conditions, such as kidney disease, liver disease, and Hodgkin lymphoma, which are more common in people with eczema.
People with eczema are more likely to experience chronic sinusitis due to the underlying inflammation and immune system dysfunction that can contribute to sinus infections and inflammation.

Gastroesophageal reflux disease, or GERD, can occur in people with eczema due to the increased sensitivity of their skin and the potential for food and stomach acid to trigger eczema symptoms and exacerbate GERD symptoms.

preparation_list.description: The cream preparation of a decoction of the herb Aloe barbadensis, rich in aloin and other anti-inflammatory compounds, helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and promoting wound healing in the affected skin.

A salve preparation of the herb Calendula officinalis, containing triterpenoids and other anti-inflammatory compounds, helps with eczema by protecting the skin and reducing inflammation in the affected areas.

The tincture preparation of the herb Echinacea purpurea, rich in alkylamides and other anti-inflammatory compounds, helps with eczema by modulating the immune response and reducing inflammation in the affected skin.

The ointment preparation of the herb St. John's Wort (Hypericum perforatum), containing hyperforin and other anti-inflammatory compounds, helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and promoting wound healing in the affected skin.

The infusion preparation of the herb Chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla), rich in apigenin and other anti-inflammatory compounds, helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation in the affected skin.

The capsule preparation of the herb Turmeric (Curcuma longa), containing curcumin and other anti-inflammatory compounds, helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and modulating the immune response in the affected skin.

The tea preparation of the herb Ginger (Zingiber officinale), rich in gingerols and other anti-inflammatory compounds, helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation in the affected skin.

The poultice preparation of the herb Plantain (Plantago major), containing aucubin and other anti-inflammatory compounds, helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and promoting wound healing in the affected skin.

The gel preparation of the herb Aloe vera (Aloe barbadensis), rich in aloin and other anti-inflammatory compounds, helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and promoting wound healing in the affected skin.

The suppository preparation of the herb Evening Primrose (Oenothera biennis), containing gamma-linolenic acid and other anti-inflammatory compounds, helps with eczema by reducing inflammation and promoting wound healing in the affected skin.
